title: Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Unwanted Behaviors Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior. Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior.

pages.text:
162
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21   (I. E. Worksheet 15)
Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping
Unwanted Behaviors
Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior.
Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior.
Examples:
Satiation = Providing relief or what is wanted before the behavior occurs.
Satiation reduces motivation for behavior and thus decreases its frequency.
Examples:
Punishment = An aversive consequence that decreases a behavior.
Behavior is decreased by consequences the person dislikes or will work to avoid.
Examples:
Behavior is decreased by consequences that stop or reduce something positive.
Examples:
Behavior is decreased when something the person wants is withheld until harmful effects of
problem behaviors are corrected and overcorrected.
Examples:
•• Be sure that punishment is specific, is time-limited, and fits the “crime.”
•• Avoid a punitive tone; let the consequence do the work.
•• If a natural punishment occurs, don’t undo it. Don’t add arbitrary punishment.
Be sure to reinforce alternative behavior to replace behavior you want stopped.
•• Extinction and punishment weaken or suppress behavior, but do not eliminate it.
•• Extinction and punishment do not teach new behavior.
•• To keep a behavior from resurfacing, reinforce an alternative behavior.
•• Punishment works only when the punisher is (or is likely to be) present.
•• Punishment leads to avoidance of the person punishing.

full_text:
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 21   (I. E. Worksheet 15)
Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping
Unwanted Behaviors
Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior.
Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior.
Examples:
Satiation = Providing relief or what is wanted before the behavior occurs.
Satiation reduces motivation for behavior and thus decreases its frequency.
Examples:
Punishment = An aversive consequence that decreases a behavior.
Behavior is decreased by consequences the person dislikes or will work to avoid.
Examples:
Behavior is decreased by consequences that stop or reduce something positive.
Examples:
Behavior is decreased when something the person wants is withheld until harmful effects of
problem behaviors are corrected and overcorrected.
Examples:
•• Be sure that punishment is specific, is time-limited, and fits the “crime.”
•• Avoid a punitive tone; let the consequence do the work.
•• If a natural punishment occurs, don’t undo it. Don’t add arbitrary punishment.
Be sure to reinforce alternative behavior to replace behavior you want stopped.
•• Extinction and punishment weaken or suppress behavior, but do not eliminate it.
•• Extinction and punishment do not teach new behavior.
•• To keep a behavior from resurfacing, reinforce an alternative behavior.
•• Punishment works only when the punisher is (or is likely to be) present.
•• Punishment leads to avoidance of the person punishing.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Strategies for Decreasing or Stopping Unwanted Behaviors Extinction = Stopping an ongoing reinforcement of behavior. Extinction leads first to a burst of behavior, and then to a decrease in behavior.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
